responses.strategic_rationale: The project faces core tensions between speed of data capture and depth of physiological measurement, between aggressive recruitment and participant safety/privacy, and between open scientific sharing and stringent regulatory compliance. The five levers strategically address footprint planning, recruitment discipline, data governance, event-triage workflows, and data quality to balance speed, cost, scope, and scientific rigor. They enable phased expansion, diversified enrollment, privacy-preserving data integration, scalable event adjudication, and robust phenotyping with transparent governance. This combination aims to produce defensible methods validation and phenotyping results that can underpin subsequent grant proposals and interventions while preserving participant trust and ethical standards.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Residence Capacity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: The operational footprint and staffing plan are aligned with the chosen capacity to ensure safe occupancy and workflow. Systemic: Capacity expands from 8 to 12 suites, boosting nightly throughput by 50% and driving approximately 12% higher per-participant operating costs. Strategic: Accelerates enrollment and data yield for Aim 2, increasing statistical power, but increases long-term cost pressure and potential staffing bottlenecks if recruitment lags. Trade-off: This expansion accelerates data collection (speed) at the expense of higher ongoing costs and staffing risk if recruitment underperforms.
options: Maintain the 8-suite footprint and phase expansion only after the pilot confirms safety and data quality.
Scale to 12 suites with enhanced night coverage and modular staffing plans to support higher throughput.
Pivot to modular, privacy-preserving care pods deployed across a network via a data-sharing service with on-demand analytics delivered to partner sites.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term regulatory changes and funding volatility.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Recruitment & Consent Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Expand outreach and streamline consent to reduce pre-screening bottlenecks and maintain ethical rigor. Systemic: Enrollment rate per month increases from roughly 2 to 3.5 participants, enlarging the eligible pool and improving representativeness. Strategic: Broadens generalizability and resilience against site-specific shocks but raises oversight complexity and potential variability in screening stringency. Trade-off: Faster recruitment may dilute sample consistency and complicate protocol adherence.
options: Maintain a tightly controlled single-site recruitment with stringent pre-admission screening and comprehensive consent.
Broaden recruitment to regional sleep clinics and neurologists with streamlined consent and remote pre-screening to boost enrollment.
Launch a dynamic, remote-friendly recruitment engine with adaptive inclusion criteria and community partnerships, financed by a pay-for-performance recruitment service.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant adherence and potential burnout or attrition due to remote recruitment.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Data Governance & Sharing Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Introduce de-identified pipelines and strict access controls with local storage and clinician-scored outcomes to minimize exposure. Systemic: Privacy incidents decline by ~40% while data processing overhead increases by ~15%, reflecting governance tightening and audit requirements. Strategic: Enables broader collaborations and secondary analyses, but imposes tighter governance and potential delays in data release and external access. Trade-off: Privacy/security versus data accessibility and speed to analysis.
options: Implement de-identified pipelines and strict access controls with local storage and offline scoring.
Adopt a multi-tier data governance model with consent management and controlled external access to support broader collaboration.
Implement privacy-preserving federated analytics across a multi-site consortium with a pay-per-use analytics service.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider data governance overhead and potential misalignment with university policies.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Event-Triage & Scoring Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Establish a baseline triage workflow with manual scoring augmented by limited automated cues to reduce reviewer burden. Systemic: Time to adjudicate events improves by ~30% with a marginal 2–3% increase in false positives, affecting precision temporarily. Strategic: Improves throughput and reduces manual workload, but introduces a risk of misclassification that could bias downstream phenotyping. Trade-off: Throughput versus accuracy.
options: Maintain full manual scoring by two independent raters and pilot limited automated cues.
Deploy a rule-based triage engine to flag probable episodes for expedited review, reducing reviewer workload.
Deploy on-device, privacy-preserving triage with edge computation and crowdsourced adjudication, offered as a pay-per-analysis service.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider the reliability of edge devices and data latency.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Data Quality & Dissemination Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Implement standardized calibration and enhanced training to stabilize annotation quality. Systemic: Inter-rater reliability improves from κ≈0.75 to ≈0.85, and nightly annotation throughput increases by ~20%, enabling faster turnaround for Aim 2. Strategic: Higher-quality datasets support robust phenotyping and more credible publications, but require additional upfront time and ongoing calibration costs. Trade-off: Quality versus speed/cost.
options: Maintain strict annotation training and two independent raters, with de-identified data deposition.
Invest in calibration workshops and standardized guidelines to improve inter-rater reliability and throughput.
Establish a federated, hardware-accelerated annotation lab with a pay-for-participation annotation consortium and open metadata sharing.
levers.review_lever: Controls data richness vs. privacy and safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider data access for external collaborators and risk of data leakage.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-site privacy governance and long-term funding resilience for multi-site collaborations. Add 'privacy-preserving federated analytics with a multi-site data governance consortium and pay-per-use analytics service' to NREM Parasomnia Data Governance Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project must navigate five core tensions: depth of physiological data versus throughput; participant safety/privacy versus experimental freedom; on-site control versus remote collaboration; data accessibility versus governance complexity; and funding stability versus rapid scalability. The five new levers introduce longitudinal phenotyping, residential safety optimization, sensing infrastructure resilience, adaptive recruitment modeling, and data transparency governance to balance these tensions while enabling phased growth, robust phenotyping, and credible dissemination.

levers.name: NREM Parasomnia Longitudinal Phenotyping Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Data harmonization across sites begins to enable standardized phenotype definitions during analyses → Systemic: Cross-site comparability improves by 20% while coordination overhead increases by 12% due to governance requirements, trading off speed of data exchange for consistency → Strategic: Enables broader multi-site validation and generalizability but increases long-term management costs and logistical complexity.
options: Maintain the current single-site phenotyping cadence with fixed nightly monitoring and centralized scoring to preserve data integrity.
Scale to regional harmonization by deploying standardized protocols and cross-site scoring guidelines to improve comparability.
Launch a privacy-preserving distributed analytics ecosystem that licenses aggregated phenotyping results to external researchers on a pay-per-use basis.
levers.review_lever: Controls cross-site comparability vs. coordination overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term regulatory changes and funding volatility.

levers.name: Residential Safety & Domestic Comfort Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Participant safety and comfort during nocturnal monitoring improve due to enhanced domestic safety measures → Systemic: Incident rate declines by 40% and nightly operating costs rise by 6% due to enhanced safety protocols, trading off reduced risk against higher expenses → Strategic: Over time, improved safety and comfort boost recruitment and retention but require sustained budget allocations.
options: Preserve domestic ambience with enhanced safety features and continuous staff oversight to minimize intrusion.
Reconfigure space into clearly separated clinical zones for safety and privacy while preserving the domestic feel.
Deploy a privacy-preserving smart-safety retrofit with edge-enabled sensors and modular care pods that can be deployed off-site if needed.
levers.review_lever: Controls participant safety vs. budgetary discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider recruitment attrition and regulatory oversight fatigue.

levers.name: Sensing Infrastructure Resilience Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Core sensing setup becomes more reliable through standardized calibration and added redundancy checks → Systemic: Data completeness improves by 15% while maintenance/calibration workload increases by 12%, trading off data integrity for operational burden → Strategic: Long-term data quality supports robust phenotyping and publication while requiring ongoing calibration resources.
options: Consolidate sensing into a core calibrated set to reduce maintenance complexity while preserving critical signals.
Implement cross-site sensor calibration across collaborating sites to improve data quality and reduce drift.
Adopt a privacy-preserving distributed sensing fabric with on-device processing and a pay-per-use access model for external researchers.
levers.review_lever: Controls privacy vs data accessibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-site legal compliance changes and vendor lock-in.

levers.name: Adaptive Recruitment & Retention Modeling Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Recruitment processes become more efficient via remote pre-screening and consent workflows → Systemic: Enrollment rate rises to 3.5 participants per month and screening time per participant falls by 25%, but oversight complexity increases by 15% → Strategic: Accelerates cohort assembly and generalizability but requires stronger governance and training, increasing ongoing cost.
options: Maintain a tightly controlled single-site recruitment with stringent pre-admission screening and comprehensive consent.
Pilot remote pre-screening and consent workflows with streamlined ethics approvals to boost enrollment while preserving rigor.
Launch a community-based, decentralized enrollment network with performance-based funding and dynamic inclusion criteria to maximize scale.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment speed vs. sample representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider participant burnout and attrition risk due to remote or high-pressure recruitment.

levers.name: Data Transparency & Stakeholder Engagement Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: De-identified datasets are prepared for controlled release, improving transparency → Systemic: Public outputs increase by 25% and external data-access requests rise by 40%, trading off governance overhead for broader engagement → Strategic: Strengthens legitimacy and opportunities for collaboration but requires robust governance and ongoing privacy safeguards.
options: Publish de-identified datasets on a controlled-access basis to support replication while preserving privacy.
Institute quarterly open briefings for participants, partners, and regulators to improve transparency and governance.
Launch a privacy-preserving, multi-site data sharing framework with paid access for external analyses.
levers.review_lever: Controls data openness vs privacy protections. Weakness: The options fail to consider misinterpretation risk and need for standard metadata schemas.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is cross-site governance for privacy-compliant data sharing and long-term multi-site funding resilience. Add 'privacy-preserving federated analytics ecosystem with multi-site governance and pay-per-use access for aggregated phenotyping results' to Sensing Infrastructure Resilience Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: The project expands decision space beyond the initial plan by introducing modular footprint optimization, cross-site phenotyping alignment, distributed annotation, edge-based triage, and participant-centered enrollment. These levers address speed, privacy, governance, data quality, and funding resilience to support phased growth, credible methods validation, and multi-site collaboration without compromising safety or ethics.

levers.name: Residential Monitoring Modularity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: The footprint becomes modular to accommodate rapid reconfiguration → Systemic: Capacity grows from 8 to 12 suites, delivering a 50% increase in nightly throughput while operating costs rise by about 8% per cycle → Strategic: Accelerates data yield for longitudinal phenotyping but heightens budget exposure if recruitment slows. It preserves safety and privacy by design but adds ongoing maintenance and supply-chain risk. Trade-offs: speed of data capture versus cost discipline.
options: Maintain the current on-site footprint and central scoring to preserve reliability and avoid disruption.
Scale to modular capacity with standardized automation of routine scoring and adaptive staffing to increase throughput.
Deploy a radical on-device federated analytics network with a subscription-based researcher access model to enable privacy-preserving cross-site phenotyping.
levers.review_lever: Controls speed of data capture vs cost discipline. Weakness: The options fail to consider supply-chain resilience and long-term maintenance costs under extended operating conditions.

levers.name: Cross-Institutional Phenotyping Alignment Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Initiates cross-site data harmonization and joint standardization of scoring → Systemic: Cross-site phenotyping consistency improves by 20% while governance overhead increases by 12% to manage policy differences → Strategic: Enables robust external validation and generalizability but raises long-term coordination costs. This requires dedicated liaisons and regular protocol updates. Trade-offs: breadth and comparability versus administrative burden.
options: Maintain strict single-site standardization with internal scoring and governance.
Implement cross-site scoring guidelines and liaison roles to improve comparability while increasing overhead.
Launch a radical interoperability platform with a subscription-based access for external researchers to enable shared phenotyping across sites.
levers.review_lever: Controls cross-site comparability vs administrative burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider evolving regulatory landscapes.

levers.name: Federated Annotation & Analytics Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Annotation migrates toward distributed processing with edge-enabled workflows → Systemic: Cross-site scoring consistency improves by 15% while data latency increases by 5–7% due to synchronization → Strategic: Enables scalable validation and external collaborations but requires governance and ongoing maintenance. Latency and synchronization must be mitigated with robust tooling. Trade-off: accessibility versus governance overhead.
options: Maintain centralized annotation with local data storage to minimize system changes.
Introduce semi-automated annotation with server-based scoring and cross-site checks to boost throughput.
Deploy a radical on-device federated analytics network and a subscription-based researcher access model to enable privacy-preserving cross-site phenotyping.
levers.review_lever: Controls data accessibility vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider latency and synchronization challenges.

levers.name: Edge-Computing Triage & Auto-Scoring Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Triage processing gains local processing to expedite flags → Systemic: Reviewer workload decreases by ~30% but false positives rise by 2–3% due to automation → Strategic: Improves throughput and reduces labor costs but risks misclassifications that could bias phenotyping. Validation and continuous monitoring are required. Trade-off: throughput versus accuracy.
options: Maintain the current triage workflow with full manual scoring to maximize accuracy.
Incorporate edge-based auto-scoring cues and lightweight triage to reduce reviewer load.
Deploy radical on-device triage with privacy-preserving technology and a pay-per-analysis model for external researchers.
levers.review_lever: Controls throughput vs accuracy. Weakness: The options fail to consider validation of edge devices in varying clinical conditions.

levers.name: Participant-Centric Enrollment & Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Consent and enrollment workflows become more participant-friendly, enabling remote pre-screening → Systemic: Enrollment rate rises by 2–3 participants per month and dropout declines by 10–15%, but oversight complexity grows by ~15% due to remote interactions → Strategic: Improves cohort representativeness and retention but demands stronger governance and privacy safeguards. Additional considerations include consent fatigue and participant burnout. Trade-off: recruitment speed versus privacy/regulatory burden.
options: Maintain current enrollment with in-hospital consent and fixed compensation.
Enhance engagement with flexible scheduling and remote pre-screening while preserving ethics.
Launch a radical consent and retention platform with mobile-native privacy-preserving data capture and dynamic compensation, supported by external research partnerships.
levers.review_lever: Controls recruitment speed vs privacy/regulatory burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and participant burnout due to remote engagement.

responses.summary: One critical missing dimension is sustainable cross-site governance for privacy-preserving data sharing and long-term multi-site funding resilience. Add 'privacy-preserving federated analytics framework with cross-site governance and pay-per-use analytics access' to Cross-Institutional Phenotyping Alignment Strategy.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: [a specific, domain-relevant second-order impact with a measurable indicator, such as a % change, capacity shift, or cost delta]"
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts that are specific to this project's domain (e.g., "[Domain]-[Decision Type] Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:", "Conservative:", "Moderate:", "Radical:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
